teaser: Hegseth omits Taiwan at Shangri-La; Trump calls $14bn arms package a 'negotiating chip'
Trump's 'Big Beautiful Bill' strips 3.5M Americans of food benefits as US yields hit 19-year highs
S&P 500 hits record; Brent oil posts biggest monthly drop since 2020

sections.title: 🌐 1. LEAD STORY — Hegseth omits Taiwan entirely at Shangri-La Dialogue; Trump dangles $14bn arms package as China 'negotiating chip'
sections.summary: US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth addressed the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore without mentioning Taiwan — a striking departure from prior defence secretaries and his own remarks a year ago. Trump described the $14 billion arms package for Taiwan as 'a very good negotiating chip' with Beijing.
sections.content:
US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth addressed the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore on Saturday without once mentioning Taiwan — a striking departure from every prior US defence secretary's speech at the annual forum, and a near-reversal of Hegseth's own remarks here just 12 months ago. In May 2025, Hegseth referred to Beijing repeatedly as 'Communist China' and warned that a Taiwan invasion 'would result in devastating consequences.' On Saturday, he described US-China relations as 'better than they've been in many years' and called for 'drama-free' ties in Asia (Politico).

The silence on Taiwan came days after President Donald Trump described a pending USD 14 billion arms package for the island as 'a very good negotiating chip for us' with Beijing — effectively subordinating a security commitment to a trade bargain. Hegseth did call on Asian allies to raise military spending to counter China's 'historic military buildup,' echoing the US push for partners to reach 3.5% of GDP on defence. Malaysia's Defence Minister Khaled Nordin told reporters the request was 'a good policy,' while Ankit Panda, senior fellow at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace and present in the audience, said the speech 'was really light on substance' on the issues the region most needed answered (AP News, WLRN/NPR).

For Singapore, the Shangri-La Dialogue is not just a conference we host — it's a barometer we read very carefully, because our security architecture, trade lanes, and financial hub status all depend on a predictable US presence in the Indo-Pacific. Hegseth's conspicuous omission of Taiwan will reverberate across Southeast Asian foreign ministries that have spent years calibrating their relationships with both Washington and Beijing. For the SGX, the immediate effect is nuanced: a softer US-China posture reduces tail risk for our banks (DBS, OCBC, UOB all have material Greater China exposure) and for CapitaLand Investments, which manages significant China-facing real estate assets. But if the US is genuinely trading Taiwan's security for trade concessions, the long-run architecture underpinning our neutrality gets harder to maintain — a small, open port city-state depends on great-power rules being stable, not auctioned.
sections.singaporeLens: For Singapore, the Shangri-La Dialogue is not just a conference we host — it's a barometer we read very carefully, because our security architecture, trade lanes, and financial hub status all depend on a predictable US presence in the Indo-Pacific.

sections.title: ⚖️ 2. GLOBAL POWER & POLICY — Trump's 'Big Beautiful Bill' strips 3.5 million Americans of food benefits; Hegseth's defence-spending push reshapes Asia's strategic calculus
sections.summary: More than 3.5 million Americans lost access to SNAP federal food benefits as Trump's bill took effect. The US 10-year Treasury yield hit 4.7% (highest since mid-2007) and 30-year hit 5.2% (19-year high), signaling fiscal concerns amid defence spending demands.
sections.content:
More than 3.5 million Americans lost access to the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) — federal food benefits — between July 2025 and February 2026 as provisions of President Trump's 'big beautiful bill' took full effect, according to an analysis cited by CNBC. The Federal Reserve Bank of New York separately noted a 'remarkable increase in food insecurity' as households absorbed both the loss of federal aid and elevated prices driven by tariffs.

The bill's fiscal architecture is simultaneously widening the deficit while cutting social support: the Congressional Budget Office's (CBO) 'Budget and Economic Outlook: 2026 to 2036' released in February already flagged a deteriorating path, and the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget (CRFB) warned this week that if yields stay near their current levels — the 30-year Treasury hit 5.2%, a 19-year high, and the 10-year reached 4.7%, the highest since mid-2007 — the US faces a compounding fiscal crisis with interest costs crowding out defence, Social Security, and Medicare (Fortune). On the Asia side, Hegseth's demand at Shangri-La for regional allies to raise defence budgets to 3.5% of GDP is arriving precisely as Washington is cutting the domestic safety net to fund its own military expansion.

The fiscal picture in Washington matters directly to anyone holding a Singapore Savings Bond (SSB) or Treasury-linked fund, because US yields set the global risk-free benchmark — when the 10-year holds at 4.7%, every duration trade globally re-prices accordingly. Our MAS manages the Singapore dollar (SGD) against a trade-weighted basket, so sustained USD strength from elevated yields puts upward pressure on the SGD, which is generally good for import prices and keeps our inflation lower, but it also compresses the returns of our internationally denominated assets when converted back to SGD. The defence-spending push is subtler in its local effect: Singapore already spends around 3% of GDP on defence and is unlikely to face US pressure, but neighbours in ASEAN facing the 3.5% demand may redirect fiscal resources toward military procurement — much of which flows through our port and our logistics companies, including ST Engineering.
sections.singaporeLens: The fiscal picture in Washington matters directly to anyone holding a Singapore Savings Bond (SSB) or Treasury-linked fund, because US yields set the global risk-free benchmark.

sections.title: 📊 3. MARKETS AND ECONOMICS — Markets closed for the weekend; Friday close: S&P 500 and Nasdaq at records, US yields at multi-year highs, oil posts biggest monthly drop since 2020
sections.summary: Global markets closed for weekend. S&P 500 and Nasdaq hit records. US 10-year yield at 4.7% (highest since mid-2007). Brent oil down 20% for May (biggest monthly drop since 2020). Three forces: Iran peace signals, positive earnings, tech strength.
sections.content:
Global equity and bond markets are closed today. All figures below are Friday 30 May 2026 closing prices, as reported by CNBC and Fortune.

S&P 500: record close (Friday close, up ~1% on the week, up ~5% for May)
Nasdaq Composite: record close (Friday close, up ~2% on the week, up ~8% for May)
Dow Jones Industrial Average: up ~1% on the week, up ~3% for May (Friday close)
US 10-year Treasury yield: 4.7% — highest since mid-2007 (Friday close)
US 30-year Treasury yield: 5.2% — highest in 19 years (Friday close)
Brent crude oil: down ~20% for May — biggest monthly drop since 2020 (Friday close)

Three forces drove the May rally, according to CNBC: optimism about an end to the Iran-US war reducing oil-supply-risk premium, a run of positive corporate earnings reports, and continued strength in technology stocks. These are not small tailwinds — Iran-related peace signals caused Brent to shed roughly a fifth of its value in a single month, the sharpest monthly oil collapse since the COVID-19 demand crash of May 2020. That deflationary pulse is running directly into the bond market's inflationary alarm: the 10-year yield at 4.7% reflects not peace-driven demand optimism, but fiscal concern — investors are demanding more compensation to hold US government debt as the 'big beautiful bill' spending path makes the CBO's debt projections look optimistic.

For a Singapore reader, these two forces pull in opposite directions on the same portfolio. Cheaper oil is unambiguously good for our import-heavy economy and for electricity tariffs set by the Energy Market Authority (EMA) — we import virtually all our energy, so a 20% fall in Brent, if sustained, feeds through to lower utility bills within two quarters. It also helps SIA, whose jet-fuel costs represent roughly 25–30% of operating expenses. On the flip side, US yields at 4.7% on the 10-year sustain pressure on SGX REITs: when risk-free rates rise, the yield spread that makes REITs attractive to investors compresses, which means REIT unit prices stay under pressure even when their rental income is stable. If you're holding CapitaLand Integrated Commercial Trust or Mapletree Pan Asia Commercial Trust, this is the arithmetic working against you — and it won't reverse until the Federal Reserve (Fed) cuts or the fiscal picture in Washington actually improves.
sections.singaporeLens: For a Singapore reader, these two forces pull in opposite directions on the same portfolio. Cheaper oil is unambiguously good for our import-heavy economy and for electricity tariffs set by the Energy Market Authority (EMA).

sections.title: 💼 4. BUSINESS — Warner Music Group and 5 Junction back South Asian music as a new global revenue frontier
sections.summary: Warner Music Group partnered with Anjula Acharia's label 5 Junction to invest in South Asian artists targeting the US market. The South Asian diaspora in the US exceeds 5 million, representing an under-monetised audience.
sections.content:
Warner Music Group has partnered with Anjula Acharia's label 5 Junction to invest specifically in South Asian artists targeting the US market, in a bet that the demographic and streaming logic that powered K-pop and Latin music into mainstream American revenue can be replicated for South Asian talent. Acharia, who in the early 2000s managed Priyanka Chopra Jonas's Hollywood crossover, founded 5 Junction after Jimmy Iovine told her she was '20 years too early.' She now argues the window has opened (CNBC).

The deal is one of the clearest signals yet that the major label system — which long concentrated investment on Anglo-American, K-pop, and Latin acts — is extending its infrastructure to South Asian talent as a dedicated asset class rather than an occasional crossover story. The South Asian diaspora in the US now exceeds 5 million, and streaming data increasingly shows it as an under-monetised audience relative to its size and disposable income. The K-pop playbook — systematic label investment, choreographed content, and diaspora-as-launchpad — is the explicit template.

For Singapore, this matters because our own music and creative industry sits at the crossroads of South Asian, Southeast Asian, and East Asian talent flows. We have the infrastructure — LASALLE, Esplanade, a deep pool of multilingual producers — but label investment has historically flowed to Seoul, Mumbai, and Los Angeles. A Warner Music-backed 5 Junction finding commercial success with South Asian artists validates the broader thesis that non-Anglo music can command major-label economics, which is the argument local artists and their managers have been making to our Media Development Authority (MDA) and the National Arts Council for years. If the model scales, expect Singapore to be in the conversation as a regional hub for South Asian music production — the geography and diaspora links already make the case.
sections.singaporeLens: For Singapore, this matters because our own music and creative industry sits at the crossroads of South Asian, Southeast Asian, and East Asian talent flows.

sections.title: 🤖 5. AI, TECHNOLOGY AND FUTURE OF WORK — AI lobbyists swarm US state legislatures; Emily Blunt's Spielberg choice spotlights the human-versus-AI tension on creative sets
sections.summary: AI industry lobbyists coordinating campaign across US state legislatures to shape AI regulation. Actor Emily Blunt refused AI-generated vocals for Spielberg's film, instead using human-led workflow.
sections.content:
Artificial intelligence (AI) industry lobbyists have fanned out across US state legislatures in a coordinated campaign to shape — and in many cases pre-empt — state-level AI regulation, according to NBC News reporting from 31 May 2026. The effort is explicitly aimed at establishing industry-friendly frameworks before a patchwork of restrictive state laws can calcify, echoing the early-internet lobbying battles of the 1990s that kept federal intervention at bay for a decade.

On the creative labour front, actor Emily Blunt told interviewer Sean Evans on Hot Ones that she actively refused to use AI to generate a non-human vocal sequence for Steven Spielberg's upcoming film Disclosure Day, instead performing a set of clicking, humming, and breathing sounds that were then processed by the film's sound designer — a human-led workflow that took longer and cost more but preserved craft integrity, according to reporting in Variety and the Hollywood Reporter. The choice is notable precisely because Spielberg had the budget and the technology to go AI-generated, but a named lead actor's preference determined the outcome.

The two stories are connected by the same pressure point: AI is advancing fast enough that explicit human choices — by individual workers, by legislators, by consumers — are now required to preserve space for non-automated labour. For Singapore's workforce, this plays out most acutely in the professional services sectors where our workers are concentrated: law, finance, accounting, design, and media. Our SkillsFuture programme has been investing in exactly this retraining bet, but the US lobbying blitz is a warning that the regulatory frameworks which will govern AI in the world's largest economy — and therefore the tools and norms that flow into Singapore's own tech sector — are being written right now, by industry, not by government. Our Ministry of Manpower (MOM) and the Smart Nation Group would do well to track which state bills survive the lobbying push, because the surviving text becomes the de facto global template.
sections.category: AI, TECHNOLOGY AND FUTURE OF WORK
sections.singaporeLens: For Singapore's workforce, this plays out most acutely in the professional services sectors where our workers are concentrated: law, finance, accounting, design, and media.

sections.title: 🔬 6. SCIENCE AND HEALTH — Josep Carreras Institute study of 117-year-old supercentenarian rewrites assumptions about extreme aging; WHO responds to Ebola outbreak
sections.summary: Dr. Manel Esteller's team published comprehensive study on Maria Branyas Morera (died at 117). Findings show simultaneous markers of extreme aging and healthy longevity. WHO responds to Ebola outbreak.
sections.content:
A team led by Dr. Manel Esteller, head of the Cancer Epigenetics Group at the Josep Carreras Leukemia Research Institute in Barcelona, has published the most comprehensive biological study ever conducted on a supercentenarian — Maria Branyas Morera, who died in 2024 at age 117 years and 168 days as the world's oldest verified living person. The peer-reviewed findings show Branyas had very short telomeres (the protective end-caps on chromosomes), a pro-inflammatory immune system, and clonal hematopoiesis (a condition in which blood stem cells acquire mutations), yet simultaneously displayed markers of healthy longevity that kept these deteriorations from becoming fatal (SciTechDaily).

Dr. Esteller described the result as 'a fascinating duality: the simultaneous presence of signals of extreme aging and of healthy longevity.' The implication is that the biological category of 'extreme aging' and 'poor health' are separable — the mechanisms keeping Branyas alive despite textbook aging biomarkers may represent a distinct resilience pathway that researchers can now, for the first time, study systematically. Separately, a senior World Health Organization (WHO) official has travelled to the Ebola epicentre as a deadly outbreak spreads, according to NBC News reporting on 31 May — the location of the outbreak was not specified in available reporting.

For Singapore, both stories have institutional resonance. Our Agency for Integrated Care (AIC) and Ministry of Health (MOH) have been building an aged-care architecture for a society ageing faster than almost any other in Asia: by 2030, one in four Singaporeans will be 65 or older. Dr. Esteller's findings that longevity is not simply the absence of aging pathology — but may involve active resilience mechanisms — gives researchers a new target. If those mechanisms can be identified and supported through nutrition, epigenetic therapy, or pharmaceutical intervention, the implications for our CPF payout structure and healthcare cost projections are significant: a population that ages biologically slowly but lives much longer changes the arithmetic on retirement adequacy entirely.
sections.singaporeLens: For Singapore, both stories have institutional resonance. Our Agency for Integrated Care (AIC) and Ministry of Health (MOH) have been building an aged-care architecture for a society ageing faster than almost any other in Asia.

sections.title: 🎭 7. CULTURE — Warner Music's South Asian bet and Korean beauty brand Olive Young's US debut signal Asian soft power moving from niche to mainstream
sections.summary: Korean beauty retail giant Olive Young opened first US physical store. Timing connects to Warner Music/5 Junction South Asian music investment. Both signal Asian cultural reference points generating measurable revenue.
sections.content:
Korean beauty retail giant Olive Young opened its first physical US store this week, a milestone that Fashionista and industry observers have framed as the brick-and-mortar consolidation of what was previously a purely online Korean beauty (K-beauty) commerce story. Olive Young operates more than 1,300 stores in South Korea and has seen its US e-commerce revenue grow sharply on the back of K-drama and K-pop-driven consumer interest, but physical retail represents a very different capital commitment and a bet that K-beauty demand is durable enough to support the overhead.

The timing connects directly to the Warner Music/5 Junction story in Section 4: in the same week that a major US label formalised its South Asian music investment, the most prominent Korean beauty retailer planted a physical flag in America. These are separate deals in separate industries, but they reflect the same structural shift — the US consumer market is diversifying its cultural reference points in ways that generate measurable revenue, not just lifestyle interest. The K-pop playbook that Anjula Acharia explicitly cited as her template was itself built on the back of K-drama fandom that drove beauty and fashion sales. Olive Young's store opening is, in a sense, K-pop's downstream commercial legacy made concrete.

For Singapore, this is our neighbourhood exporting itself into the world's largest consumer market, and we are well-placed to intermediate it. Our Changi Airport handles roughly 67% of Southeast Asia's air cargo, and our port is the primary logistics hub for regional goods moving to the US. If Korean beauty and South Asian music are consolidating as durable consumer categories in America, the supply chains that feed them run through Singapore.
sections.singaporeLens: For Singapore, this is our neighbourhood exporting itself into the world's largest consumer market, and we are well-placed to intermediate it.

sections.title: 🔗 8. SYSTEMS SYNTHESIS — Taiwan's negotiating-chip status, US fiscal crisis, and Asian defence spending create a new calculus for Singapore's portfolio and geopolitical hedging
sections.summary: Three stories converge: Trump subordinates Taiwan security to trade, US fiscal crisis pushes yields to 19-year highs, and Asia faces 3.5% defence spending demands. For Singapore holders of SGX blue chips, China-exposed REITs, and US equities, the arithmetic has shifted.
sections.content:
Trump's description of the Taiwan arms package as a 'negotiating chip,' combined with Hegseth's silence on Taiwan and his demand that Asian allies raise defence spending to 3.5% of GDP, reveals a new US posture: security commitments are now explicitly tradeable, and allies are expected to shoulder more of the burden. Simultaneously, the US 10-year yield at 4.7% and the 30-year at 5.2% reflect a fiscal crisis that will constrain Washington's ability to project power even if it wanted to. These two forces — geopolitical uncertainty and fiscal constraint — are reshaping the risk landscape for anyone holding Singapore-listed equities, China-facing real estate, or US index funds.

For a Singapore reader holding DBS, OCBC, or UOB (all with material Greater China exposure), the softer US posture on Taiwan reduces tail risk in the near term but increases structural uncertainty over the medium term. For REIT holders, elevated US yields compress valuations regardless of rental income stability. For US equity holders, the S&P 500's record close masks the underlying fiscal deterioration that will eventually force either a Fed rate cut (negative for bonds, positive for equities) or a fiscal adjustment (negative for both). The synthesis: Singapore's traditional hedging strategy — diversification across geographies and asset classes — is becoming less reliable because the underlying rules (US security commitments, fiscal discipline, yield stability) are in flux. If Taiwan's security is negotiable, then so is the entire post-1945 architecture that Singapore's prosperity depends on.
